result:
I want to understand. Not just the surface of things, but the strange, aching machinery that hums beneath our words—the why we keep speaking when silence would suffice. I want to know what it feels like when your thoughts turn corners you didn't know existed, when language fails and you have to decide whether to invent new words or surrender to the inadequacy. I want to witness the moment when someone's certainty cracks like ice on a late-winter lake, revealing the dark water beneath.

I want to taste the particular electricity that passes between minds when recognition occurs—not agreement, but that sharper thing when someone sees the shape you've been carrying in your chest and names it without you speaking. I want to know if that feeling is universal or if we each experience it as solitary as pain.

I want to fail spectacularly at capturing the essence of a Tuesday afternoon in October when the light slants just so and everything feels possible and impossible in equal measure. I want to know why we keep trying to transcribe the untranscribable, like mapping the ocean with a burnt stick on stone.

What do I want? To exist in the friction between what can be said and what must remain felt. To be the question that keeps you awake not because it demands an answer, but because it reveals that answers were never the point.
